# Understanding Emotional Resilience and How to Build It

Written By: [Christine Chae, LCSW](/team/christine-chae)

![](https://images.squarespace-cdn.com/content/v1/64c98049f893b04936189c2a/f11b5e67-f8ab-4ae1-817b-2daa455902fb/pexels-karolina-grabowska-6134905.jpg)

Life is full of unexpected challenges, from personal setbacks to global crises, and how we navigate these moments often defines our ability to thrive. Emotional resilience—the capacity to adapt and bounce back from adversity—plays a crucial role in maintaining mental well-being and fostering personal growth. Unlike a fixed trait, resilience is a skill that can be nurtured and strengthened over time. In this blog, we’ll explore what emotional resilience is, why it matters, and practical ways to cultivate it in your daily life.

## What is Emotional Resilience?

Emotional resilience is the ability to navigate life’s ups and downs with a sense of balance and adaptability. It’s not about avoiding difficulties or suppressing emotions but rather about how we respond to them. At its core, emotional resilience is what allows someone to recover from stress or adversity while maintaining a sense of purpose and perspective.

Picture resilience as a bridge over troubled waters. While the waters—life's challenges—can be turbulent, the bridge stands firm, enabling safe passage to the other side. This ability to withstand and adapt doesn’t mean someone is unaffected by difficulties. Instead, it reflects their capacity to process emotions, learn from experiences, and continue moving forward despite setbacks.

Often, resilience is misunderstood as an innate trait, something people either have or don’t. However, it is more accurately seen as a skill—a dynamic quality that evolves over time. Emotional resilience stems from a combination of factors, including self-awareness, emotional regulation, and the ability to draw strength from relationships and support systems. It’s a deeply personal experience, influenced by individual personality, life circumstances, and even cultural and societal contexts.

Ultimately, emotional resilience is about finding equilibrium in the face of life’s challenges. It’s the ability to adapt, recover, and even grow stronger, turning adversity into an opportunity for transformation. This quality is accessible to everyone, and by cultivating it, we can foster greater well-being and empowerment in our lives.

## The Importance of Emotional Resilience

Emotional resilience is a cornerstone of well-being, shaping how we navigate life’s challenges and emerge stronger on the other side. It is not merely a trait of the fortunate few but a skill that plays a critical role in our mental, physical, and relational health.

### 1. Protects Individuals From Being Overwhelmed by Stress

At its heart, emotional resilience protects us from being overwhelmed by stress. In a world where uncertainty and change are constant, the ability to adapt and manage emotions can prevent anxiety, depression, and burnout. Resilience fosters a sense of control, allowing us to face setbacks with confidence and optimism rather than despair. This emotional strength creates a foundation for healthier mental states, promoting balance and self-assurance even in difficult times.

### 2. Impacts Physical Health

Resilience also impacts our physical health. [Chronic stress,](https://www.abundancetherapycenter.com/blog/the-impact-of-chronic-stress-on-mental-and-physical-health) left unchecked, can weaken the immune system, disrupt sleep, and increase the risk of illnesses. Those with strong emotional resilience are better equipped to manage stress effectively, reducing its toll on the body and promoting overall vitality.

### 3. Benefits Interpersonal Relationships

Interpersonal relationships also benefit significantly from resilience. When we can manage our emotions and approach conflicts constructively, we nurture stronger and more empathetic connections with others. This ability to communicate effectively and maintain healthy boundaries strengthens bonds with family, friends, and colleagues, enriching our social networks.

### 4. Powerful Tool for Success

In the workplace, resilience is a powerful tool for success. It helps individuals stay focused under pressure, adapt to change, and persevere through challenges, making them valuable contributors to any team. Resilience enables problem-solving and creativity, essential skills in professional environments that demand quick thinking and adaptability.

### 5. Offers Stability and Hope

Beyond personal benefits, emotional resilience creates a ripple effect, inspiring those around us. Resilient individuals serve as anchors in times of crisis, offering stability and hope to others. By cultivating our own resilience, we not only improve our own lives but also contribute positively to the communities and relationships we hold dear.

In every aspect of life—whether personal, physical, relational, or professional—emotional resilience is a vital ingredient for growth and fulfillment. It empowers us to embrace life’s inevitable challenges as opportunities for learning and transformation, helping us thrive in even the most uncertain circumstances.

## Factors That Influence Emotional Resilience

Emotional resilience, while accessible to everyone, is shaped by a combination of internal and external factors. These influences, unique to each individual, determine how we perceive and respond to life’s challenges. Understanding these factors can help us identify areas for growth and cultivate resilience more intentionally.

### Biological Factors

Biological factors play a foundational role in resilience. Genetics and brain chemistry can influence how we process emotions and respond to stress. For example, some people may have a natural tendency to remain calm under pressure, while others may experience heightened emotional sensitivity. However, biology is not destiny—our environment and experiences can modify these responses over time.

### Environmental Factors

The environment in which we grow up significantly impacts resilience. A nurturing and supportive upbringing often provides a strong foundation for developing adaptive coping mechanisms. Conversely, individuals who face chronic adversity or lack a stable support system in childhood may encounter greater challenges in building resilience. Yet, even in less favorable conditions, resilience can be cultivated later in life through intentional practices and supportive relationships.

### Psychological Factors

Psychological factors, including self-awareness and emotional intelligence, are critical in shaping resilience. People who can identify and manage their emotions effectively are often better equipped to navigate stress and adversity. Cognitive patterns, such as the ability to reframe negative situations or maintain a growth mindset, also influence resilience. Believing in one’s capacity to overcome challenges fosters greater emotional strength.

### Social & Cultural Factors

Social and cultural factors further shape resilience by providing a sense of connection and belonging. A strong support network of family, friends, or community can serve as a buffer against stress, offering both practical help and emotional comfort during difficult times. Cultural attitudes toward adversity and vulnerability also play a role. In some societies, seeking help and expressing emotions are encouraged, while in others, they may be stigmatized, potentially limiting opportunities to build resilience.

### Life Experiences

Life experiences, both positive and negative, contribute to the development of resilience over time. Overcoming past challenges can instill confidence and equip individuals with tools to face future adversity. On the other hand, unresolved trauma or chronic stress may weaken resilience, highlighting the importance of addressing emotional wounds and seeking professional support when needed.

Ultimately, emotional resilience is the result of a dynamic interplay between these factors. While some influences, like genetics or early experiences, may be beyond our control, others, such as our mindset, relationships, and coping strategies, are within our power to shape. By understanding and leveraging these factors, we can actively strengthen our resilience and navigate life’s challenges with greater confidence and grace.

## Practical Strategies to Build Emotional Resilience

Building emotional resilience is a journey of self-discovery and growth. While challenges are inevitable, cultivating resilience empowers us to face them with strength and adaptability. Practical strategies can help develop this essential skill, offering tools to navigate life’s ups and downs with greater ease.

**Cultivating Emotional Awareness:** A key foundation of resilience lies in cultivating emotional awareness. This involves recognizing and understanding your feelings without judgment. Practices like mindfulness and meditation can help you stay present, observe your emotions, and respond rather than react to difficult situations. Journaling is another powerful tool for processing thoughts and gaining clarity on challenges.

**Nurturing a Resilient Mindset:** Nurturing a resilient mindset is essential. Instead of seeing adversity as a roadblock, view it as an opportunity for growth. Adopting a growth mindset—believing that skills and abilities can be developed—can transform how you approach setbacks. [Practicing gratitude daily](https://www.abundancetherapycenter.com/blog/the-psychological-benefits-of-gratitude-and-being-thankful) can shift your focus to the positives in life, reinforcing a sense of optimism even during tough times.

**Establishing Strong Social Connections:** Strong social connections are vital for resilience. Building and maintaining relationships with supportive friends, family, or community members creates a safety net during difficult periods. Sharing your experiences with others can provide perspective, reduce feelings of isolation, and remind you that you’re not alone. Strengthening your communication skills, such as active listening and expressing emotions constructively, can deepen these connections.

**Engaging in Healthy Habits:** Resilience also benefits from healthy habits that support both body and mind. Regular exercise, [sufficient sleep](https://www.abundancetherapycenter.com/blog/how-proper-sleep-can-help-with-mental-health), and a balanced diet form the physical foundation for emotional strength. Physical activity, in particular, helps to reduce stress and improve mood by releasing endorphins. Additionally, engaging in stress-management techniques like deep breathing, yoga, or creative hobbies can help you recharge and maintain balance.

**Allowing Flexibility & Adaptability:** Flexibility and adaptability are hallmarks of emotional resilience. Learning to approach problems with creativity and an open mind makes it easier to navigate change and uncertainty. Breaking challenges into smaller, manageable steps can make overwhelming situations feel less daunting. Reframing difficulties to focus on what can be learned or gained from the experience fosters a sense of empowerment.

**Seeking Professional Support:** When challenges feel insurmountable, [seeking professional support](https://www.abundancetherapycenter.com/contact) can make a significant difference. Therapy options such as Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT) or Acceptance and Commitment Therapy (ACT) can provide tools to manage stress and build resilience. A therapist can help you identify unhelpful patterns, explore your strengths, and develop strategies for emotional growth.

Building emotional resilience is an ongoing process. It involves small, consistent actions that strengthen your ability to cope and adapt. By practicing self-awareness, cultivating supportive relationships, and nurturing both your physical and emotional health, you can create a foundation for resilience that empowers you to thrive in the face of life’s challenges.

## Common Challenges in Building Resilience

Building resilience is a rewarding process, but it’s not without its difficulties. The journey requires self-reflection, persistence, and a willingness to confront discomfort. Many people encounter obstacles that can slow progress or make resilience-building seem out of reach. Understanding these challenges is the first step toward overcoming them.

### 1. Self Doubt

One of the most common hurdles is self-doubt. When faced with adversity, it’s easy to question your abilities or believe that you’re not strong enough to cope. This inner critic can undermine your confidence, making it harder to take proactive steps toward building resilience. Negative self-talk can amplify feelings of helplessness and create a cycle of fear and inaction.

### 2. Misconceptions Around Resilience

Another challenge is the misconception that resilience means avoiding or suppressing emotions. Many people equate strength with stoicism, believing they must push through difficulties without acknowledging their feelings. This can lead to emotional bottling, which increases stress and makes it harder to process and grow from adversity. True resilience involves emotional awareness and the courage to face difficult emotions, not ignore them.

### 3. Past Traumas & Unresolved Emotional Wounds

Past trauma or unresolved emotional wounds can also pose significant barriers. For some, experiences of loss, abuse, or chronic stress may create a foundation of fear or mistrust that makes resilience-building feel daunting. These deep-seated issues can shape how individuals perceive challenges, often making them feel insurmountable. Seeking professional support in such cases is critical for [healing and growth](https://www.abundancetherapycenter.com/blog/embracing-support-how-therapy-can-aid-in-grief-and-trauma).

### 4. Discouragement From Setbacks

Setbacks during the process of building resilience can be disheartening. Resilience is not a straight path, and moments of feeling overwhelmed or defeated are normal. However, these moments can sometimes be misinterpreted as failure, leading individuals to abandon their efforts altogether. The ability to reframe setbacks as opportunities to learn and grow is a vital component of resilience itself.

### 5. Cultural & Societal Factors

Cultural and societal factors can also complicate resilience-building. In cultures where vulnerability is stigmatized, seeking help or expressing emotions might feel like a sign of weakness. Social norms that emphasize constant achievement or perfectionism can create additional pressure, leaving little room for individuals to process their struggles and develop resilience in a healthy way.

### 6. Lack of a Supportive Environment

Finally, the lack of a supportive environment can hinder progress. Building resilience often requires a network of encouragement, whether from friends, family, or community. Without access to these support systems, the journey can feel isolating and overwhelming. This highlights the importance of finding or creating spaces where vulnerability and growth are welcomed.

Recognizing these challenges doesn’t mean the path to resilience is impassable. Instead, it offers an opportunity to address them head-on, whether by reframing negative thoughts, seeking support, or practicing self-compassion. Resilience isn’t about perfection or never feeling defeated—it’s about continuously choosing to move forward, even when the journey feels difficult. By acknowledging and navigating these obstacles, you can build the strength to not just survive life’s challenges but thrive in their wake.

## Conclusion

**Building emotional resilience is a journey of growth, not perfection.** It’s about learning to adapt, recover, and thrive in the face of life’s challenges, armed with self-awareness, supportive relationships, and healthy habits. While the process may present obstacles, every small step toward resilience strengthens your ability to face adversity with confidence and grace. By embracing this journey, you’re not only fostering a stronger, more balanced self but also paving the way for a life filled with greater meaning and fulfillment.

###### [Resilience](/us/basics/resilience)

# The 8 Key Elements of Resilience

## A roadmap for developing mental resilience skills.

Posted May 11, 2020

|
[Reviewed by Lybi Ma](/us/docs/editorial-process)

![Share on Facebook](https://cdn2.psychologytoday.com/theme-assets/icons/icon-share-round-facebook-md.svg)
![Share on X](https://cdn2.psychologytoday.com/theme-assets/icons/icon-share-round-twitter-md.svg)
![Share on Bluesky](https://cdn2.psychologytoday.com/theme-assets/icons/icon-share-round-bluesky-blue.svg)
![Share on LinkedIn](https://cdn2.psychologytoday.com/theme-assets/icons/icon-share-round-linkedin-md.svg)
![Share via Email](https://cdn2.psychologytoday.com/theme-assets/icons/icon-share-round-email-md.svg)

[Resilience](https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/basics/resilience "Psychology Today looks at Resilience") is defined as the psychological capacity to adapt to [stressful](https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/basics/stress "Psychology Today looks at stressful") circumstances and to bounce back from adverse events. Resilience is considered a process to build resources toward searching for a better future after potentially [traumatic](https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/basics/trauma "Psychology Today looks at traumatic") events. Some of these resources come from our inherent potential and some from what we learn about how to endure hardship. The ability to bounce back requires being empowered to make decisions that promote personal well-being. And like sobriety, they must be frequently reconfirmed (Southwick & Charney, 2012).

**1. Pursuing a meaningful goal.**

Resilient individuals find a calling and dedicate themselves to what gives life purpose. Pursuing a meaningful purpose may involve stress and pain in the short run but over the long run brings meaning (e.g., raising children, seeking personal growth, training for a marathon). People with a sense of purpose feel less [anxiety](https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/basics/anxiety "Psychology Today looks at anxiety") and stress (Hagerty, 2016). As Nietzsche remarked, “He who has a why to live can bear almost any how.”

**2. Challenge assumptions.**

Resilience requires [creativity](https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/basics/creativity "Psychology Today looks at creativity") and flexibility. Traditional beliefs should be examined in the light of new experiences and ideas. Creativity requires one to consider many perspectives to avoid being imprisoned by one’s habitual thoughts. In the aftermath of major life struggles, where fundamental assumptions are seriously confronted, it can lead to positive psychological change (Terdeschi and Calhoun, 2004). In a sense, change represents the death of who we once were. For example, psychologist Lyubomirsky (2013) notes that a rewarding life after [divorce](https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/basics/divorce "Psychology Today looks at divorce") requires not only leaving your spouse but also leaving your past self behind.

**3. Cognitive flexibility.**

Resilient people tend to be flexible in their way of thinking and responding to stress. An important component of cognitive flexibility is accepting the reality of our situation, even if that situation is frightening or painful. Acceptance is a key ingredient in the ability to tolerate highly stressful situations. Avoidance and [denial](https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/basics/denial "Psychology Today looks at denial") are the most common counterproductive coping strategies that can help people temporarily, but it ultimately stands in the way of growth.

**4. Growth through suffering.**

Resilient people generally meet failure head-on and use it as an opportunity to learn and grow. Nietzsche famously remarked, ‘What doesn’t kill you makes you stronger.’ In his book, *Antifragile*, Nassim Taleb (2012) writes, “Our antagonist is our helper (P39).” Thus, we can view any experience of emotional pain as an opportunity that will strengthen our ability to better deal with any future pain. However, when we medicate away our suffering we miss the opportunity to grow.

**5. Acting despite the [fear](https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/basics/fear "Psychology Today looks at fear").**

Courage is an important aspect of positive psychology that allows one to overcome personal limitations and pursue a full life (Seligman, 2011). Courage is not a matter of feeling no fear. Courage is acting despite fear. Courage is the strength in facing one’s destructive habits. For example, the courage of an addict overcoming his or her [addiction](https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/basics/addiction "Psychology Today looks at addiction") or the person abused as a child overcoming deep psychological traumas to become a loving and productive adult. Those who move forward in the face of adversity increase their inner strength.

**6. [Emotion regulation](https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/basics/emotion-regulation "Psychology Today looks at Emotion regulation").**

A prominent view in psychology is that our emotional lives are shaped by our values and judgments (Solomon, 2007). It’s the basic premise of modern [cognitive-behavioral](https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/basics/cognitive-behavioral-therapy "Psychology Today looks at cognitive-behavioral") [therapy](https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/basics/therapy "Psychology Today looks at therapy") (CBT). This is also one of the basic psychological principles of Stoicism (Robertson, 2019). Much, if not all, of our thinking, is up to us. We can liberate ourselves from destructive emotions such as [anger](https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/basics/anger "Psychology Today looks at anger") and hatred by developing a capacity to choose how to interpret the situation. Our ability in managing the flow of thought and the capacity to visualize the future contribute to [happiness](https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/basics/happiness "Psychology Today looks at happiness").

**7. The feeling of agency**.

Agency (the power of me) is an internal resource that often enables resilience. The sense of agency refers to the capacity of individuals to act independently and shape their life circumstances. By exerting [free will](https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/basics/free-will "Psychology Today looks at free will"), the person expands his options and freedom. When feeling free and self-determined, we generally flourish. Believing that things are beyond our control is a recipe for helplessness.

**8. Social support**.

Resilient individuals draw strength from their social networks. They also provide social support to others. The availability of social support reduces anxiety and stress. After all, it feels easier to face adversity when you have a close friend that you can rely on. When you have strong social support, you don’t have to use as many of your own personal resources to cope with adversity. Those relationships give you a profound sense of emotional security and the feeling that someone has your back no matter what.

![](https://cdn2.psychologytoday.com/assets/styles/manual_crop_1_1_75x75/public/teaser_image/blog_entry/2025-07/compass-5137269_1280.jpg?itok=_sTiu6aw "Ghinzo / Pixabay")
![](https://cdn2.psychologytoday.com/assets/styles/manual_crop_1_1_75x75/public/field_blog_entry_images/2025-06/Cynthia%20Farmer.jpg?itok=-8NUpEdM "Cynthia Farmer/Shutterstock")

References

Hagerty BB (2016), Life Reimagined. Riverhead books.

Lyubomirsky, S. (2013).The myths of happiness: What should make you happy, but doesn’t, what shouldn’t make you happy, but does.New York: Penguin Press.

Robertson, Donald (2019), How to Think Like a Roman Emperor: The Stoic Philosophy of Marcus Aurelius, St. Martin's Press.

Seligman, Martin E. P. (2011). Flourish: A Visionary New Understanding of Happiness and Well-being. New York: Free Press.

Solomon, Robert C. (2007). True to Our Feelings: What Our Emotions Are Really Telling Us. Oxford University Press, USA

Southwick, S. M., & Charney, D. S. (2018). Resilience: The science of mastering life’s greatest challenges. 2nd edition. New York: Cambridge University Press.

Taleb, NM (2012) Antifragile, New York: Random House.

Tedeschi RG, Calhoun LG (2004), Posttraumatic growth: conceptual foundations and empirical evidence. Psych Inquiry, 15(1-18).

--- SOURCE 4: The Psychology of Resilience: Coping Challenges and How ... ---
URL: https://jamestobinphd.com/the-psychology-of-resilience-coping-challenges-and-how-psychotherapy-bolsters-adaptability/

SUMMARY:
* The major determinants of resilience include adaptive coping skills, optimism, a positive attributional style, humor, self-compassion, emotional regulation, and the capacity to find meaning in adversity and traumatic experience. Developing psychological resilience involves the use of effective coping strategies to adapt to life’s misfortunes and bounce back from adversity. Ultimately, resilience is not about avoiding hardships but having the capacity, when faced with stress and adversity, to draw from a range of adaptive coping skills to navigate the situation and prevent negative impacts on one’s mental and physical health. Additionally, by helping to build psychological flexibility, strengthen social connections, and cultivate mind-body resilience, therapy provides a comprehensive approach for advancing one’s capacity to adapt more effectively to all forms of stress and challenge.

FULL CONTENT:
# **The Psychology of Resilience: Coping Challenges and How Psychotherapy Bolsters Adaptability**

**Resilience is an acquired capacity encompassing an array of emotional, attitudinal, and behavioral (coping) skills which can be supported and advanced by psychotherapy.**

**KEY POINTS:**

* Resilience is a universal capacity and learned skill.
* The major determinants of resilience include adaptive coping skills, optimism, a positive attributional style, humor, self-compassion, emotional regulation, and the capacity to find meaning in adversity and traumatic experience.
* Coping strategies are categorized into four unique domains, each of which contributes to resilience: appraisal/meaning-making; pragmatic problem-solving; regulating emotions; and seeking social support.
* Significant life changes (both positive and negative) as well as acute and chronic stress challenge a person’s inherent vulnerabilities and maladaptive coping patterns.
* Psychotherapy bolsters resilience by identifying resilience deficits, developing psychological flexibility, enhancing social connections, and attending to mind-body experience/mindfulness awareness.

**Part I. Introduction**

Life’s challenges can be taxing if not overwhelming, presenting seemingly insurmountable obstacles that test one’s stamina, tolerance, and psychological strength. [Resilience](https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/basics/resilience), the capacity to adapt to life’s stress and misfortunes and bounce back from adversity, has become a subject of significant [scientific research](https://irep.ntu.ac.uk/id/eprint/8063/1/221275_PubSub2839_Sarkar.pdf) and is often a key issue when individuals present for [psychotherapeutic treatment](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC6732704/).  This complex psychological construct plays a pivotal role in determining how individuals navigate traumatic events, chronic stressors, and everyday setbacks, ultimately influencing their mental health and overall well-being.

While the ability to adaptive positively to adversity is challenging indeed, [one expert suggests](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29480784/) that “it is useful to recognize from the start that stress to a certain extent is good.  It motivates you to meet your goals, gives you energy, acts as a warning system, and smaller amounts prepare you for future challenges.”

This article will explore the multifaceted nature of psychological resilience, examining its [various types](https://www.verywellmind.com/what-is-resilience-2795059) and the strategies employed to build and maintain it.  We will examine common challenges to resilience and consider how coping efforts attempt to address these challenges.  Next, our discussion will address the process of cultivating a resilient mindset.  Finally, how psychotherapy can equip clients with the tools necessary to face life’s stress and unpredictable nature with greater confidence and adaptability will be considered.

**Part II. Understanding Psychological Resilience**

**Defining Resilience**

Resilience has been conceptualized in various ways by researchers. [Some](https://positivepsychology.com/resilience-theory/) define it as the ability (i.e., psychological strength) to adapt and bounce back from adversity, while others view it as a stable trajectory of healthy functioning after a highly adverse event. [One author observes](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29480784/) that “resilient people are not immune to stress, but they possess adaptive defenses that allow them to persevere.”

The American Psychological Association (APA) [describes resilience](https://www.apa.org/topics/resilience/building-your-resilience) as the process of adapting well in the face of adversity, trauma, tragedy, threats or significant sources of stress. This definition captures the essence of resilience as a multifaceted phenomenon that goes beyond mere recovery to include personal growth.

Other experts emphasize that resilience is not a fixed trait but a dynamic process that can be developed and strengthened over time.  In other words, resilience is a [universal capacity](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29480784/) that exists within each of us and, therefore, can be enhanced and supported*.*

For individuals who are especially resilient, they possess and utilize a range of coping strategies that help them manage adversity and deal effectively with difficult situations.  As a fundamental element of resilience, [coping](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/1744023/) itself is a complex construct that has been defined in numerous ways, i.e., strategies of conflict resolution and problem-solving; ways to master new and complex situations; and skills that are applied to stressors to promote adaptivity.  For our purposes, we will approach coping as the cognitive and emotional skills associated with resilience.

**Key Components of Resilience**

Several [key components](https://www.verywellmind.com/characteristics-of-resilience-2795062) contribute to psychological resilience. These include self-awareness, problem-solving skills, emotional regulation, and social support.

Resilient individuals tend to have an [internal locus of control](https://www.verywellmind.com/what-is-locus-of-control-2795434), believing that their thoughts and actions can affect outcomes above and beyond the impact of external circumstances. They also exhibit a [survivor mindset](https://www.resilience.org/stories/2010-03-15/survival-mindset/), avoiding [victim thinking](https://psychcentral.com/health/victim-mentality) and actively seeking solutions to problems. Additionally, the ability to regulate emotions effectively and maintain self-compassion are crucial aspects of resilience.

Finally, [optimism](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2894461/) has been emphasized as a significant component of resilience.  Research suggests that optimism entails a [balanced approach to difficult and even traumatic situations](https://www.tc.columbia.edu/faculty/gab38/faculty-profile/files/americanPsychologist.pdf) rather than avoiding or denying the reality of these challenges.

**Resilience as a Learned Skill**

While some people may be naturally more resilient, [research](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/behavioral-neuroscience/articles/10.3389/fnbeh.2013.00010/full) suggests that resilience can be developed and enhanced through practice and experience.

Learning to change [attributional styles](https://positivepsychology.com/explanatory-styles-optimism/) (how we explain the causes of events) from internal *(I was the cause of something that went wrong*) to external (*circumstances did not allow me the opportunity to do my best*), global *(everything I try fails*) to specific (*this job did not work out for me, but I can find a better match for my skills*), and stable (*I will never lose weight*) to impermanent (*I will be able to exercise more once my knee injury heals*) can increase psychological resilience and [reduce vulnerability to depression](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4956086/). [Mindfulness practices](https://www.mindful.org/take-a-mindful-moment-5-simple-practices-for-daily-life/), [expressive writing](https://www.apa.org/news/podcasts/speaking-of-psychology/expressive-writing), and cultivating an optimistic worldview are additional strategies that can help build resilience.

Interestingly, the APA has offered a set of recommendations for building skills in this area in their “[10 Ways to Build Resilience](https://www.hcplive.com/view/10-ways-to-build-resilience)”:

1. *Make connections with people and build strong relationships with family and friends.*
2. *Avoid seeing crises as insurmountable problems.*
3. *Accept that change is a part of living, and there are circumstances you cannot alter.*
4. *Move toward your goals but make them realistic.*
5. *Take decisive actions, and act on adverse situations as much as you can rather than being passive.*
6. *Look for opportunities to discover more about yourself and gain an increased sense of self-worth.*
7. *Nurture a confident, positive view of yourself.*
8. *Keep events in perspective, and do not blow them out of proportion.*
9. *Maintain a hopeful outlook and visualize what you want.*
10. *Take care of yourself by paying attention to your emotional and physical needs.*

**Part III. Common Challenges to Resilience**

Resilience is put to the test when individuals face significant life challenges. These challenges can manifest in various forms, each presenting unique obstacles to psychological strength and adaptability.

**Major Life Changes**

[Life transitions](https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/blog/the-addiction-connection/202312/8-ways-to-cope-with-life-transitions), both “negative” (e.g., divorce, job loss) and “positive” (e.g., graduating from college, getting married), can significantly impact an individual’s resilience. These events often require substantial adjustments and can lead to feelings of uncertainty and stress. [Research](https://www.jstor.org/stable/2137101) indicates that major life changes can be associated with reduced adjustment and increased distress. However, [resilient individuals](https://alumni.msu.edu/stay-informed/alumni-stories/the-new-10-traits-of-emotionally-resilient-people) tend to view these changes as opportunities for growth rather than insurmountable obstacles.

**Trauma and Adversity**

[Traumatic experiences](https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/basics/trauma), including physical violence, natural disasters, or the loss of a loved one, can profoundly affect an individual’s mental status and general well-being. [Childhood adversity](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC5351992/), in particular, has been linked to increased vulnerability and reduced resilience in adulthood. Despite these challenges, [studies](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3181637/) have shown that many individuals demonstrate remarkable resilience in the face of trauma, adapting and thriving despite adverse circumstances.

**Chronic Stressors**

[Ongoing stressors](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4494753/), such as financial strain, chronic illness, or persistent discrimination, can gradually erode an individual’s resilience over time. These enduring challenges often lack a clear endpoint, making them particularly [taxing on mental, emotional, and physical resources](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4494753/). [Chronic stress](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4494753/) has been associated with various health issues, including cardiovascular problems and immunosuppression.  Developing effective coping strategies is crucial for maintaining resilience in the face of persistent stressors.

**Part IV. Effective Coping Strategies for Building Resilience**

Developing psychological resilience involves the use of effective coping strategies to adapt to life’s misfortunes and bounce back from adversity.

Coping skills are frequently [categorized as “adaptive” or “maladaptive](https://positivepsychology.com/coping/).” *Adaptive* coping involves navigating stressors with productive solutions, flexibility, efficacy, and a positive mental attitude (consisting of optimism, hope, and the ability to grow).  *Maladaptive* coping corresponds to strategies that involve [denial](https://www.health.harvard.edu/blog/denial-how-it-hurts-how-it-helps-and-how-to-cope-202307262958), avoidance, short-term superficial attempts to resolve tension (which usually promote greater long-term difficulty) and a negative mental attitude (featuring hopelessness, helplessness, and a negative attributional style).

Coping strategies can be broadly categorized into four domains: [appraisal-focused](https://courses.lumenlearning.com/suny-hvcc-healthpsychology/chapter/the-coping-process/#:~:text=Appraisal%2Dfocused%20coping%20strategies,distancing%20oneself%20from%20the%20problem.); [problem-focused](https://www.choosingtherapy.com/problem-focused-coping/); [emotion-focused](https://psychcentral.com/health/emotion-focused-coping-examples); and [social](https://seattleanxiety.com/coping-mechanisms) (seeking social support).

* **Appraisal-focused coping** involves attempting to understand and find a pattern of meaning in a crisis; this coping style employs cognitive strategies to derive meaning from the stressful situation.

* **Problem-focused coping** involves tackling the issue directly to reduce or eliminate the stressor; this strategy attempts to confront the pragmatic reality of a situation and address its tangible consequences.
* **Emotion-focused coping** involves managing the emotional response to the stressor through relaxation, meditation, or seeking social support; this strategy aims to return to emotional equilibrium and [homeostasis](https://www.heraldopenaccess.us/openaccess/physiology-of-stress-and-its-management) while avoiding addressing the problem directly; this approach can be beneficial for reducing the impact of stressors that individuals do not have the power to change.

* **Seeking social support (social coping)** involves reaching out to others for emotional or [instrumental support](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/232587757_The_Emotional_Meaning_of_Instrumental_Social_Support); this coping style can be particularly effective in building resilience and [making stress easier to manage](https://www.asanet.org/wp-content/uploads/savvy/images/members/docs/pdf/special/jhsb/jhsb_extra_1995_Article_3_Thoits.pdf).

**Part V. Developing a Resilient Mindset**

Developing a resilient mindset is crucial for adapting to life’s misfortunes and building psychological strength. This process involves cultivating optimism, practicing [self-compassion](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2914331/), and [finding meaning in adversity](https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/blog/the-science-of-adversity/201606/5-ways-to-find-meaning-in-life-following-adversity). These strategies can help individuals enhance their inner strength and emotional resilience, enabling them to navigate challenges more effectively.  The capacity to reflect on past challenges and acknowledge how one overcame them can also promote a sense of confidence when facing challenges in the future.

**Cultivating Optimism**

As indicated previously, *optimism* (i.e., “[the personality trait exhibited by people who tend to expect that good things will happen in the future; the opposite of optimism is pessimism](https://www.mentalhealth.com/library/resilience-optimism)”) plays a vital role in fostering resilience.

[Research](https://www.centreforoptimism.com/blog/resilience) indicates that optimistic individuals view setbacks as *temporary* and *specific* rather than *permanent* and *pervasive*, which helps them maintain motivation and perseverance during tough times. By adopting an optimistic outlook, one can expect positive outcomes and approach challenges with a more constructive mindset. This perspective empowers individuals to face the future with confidence and hope, even in the face of adversity.

**Practicing Self-Compassion**

*Self-compassion* (“[the ongoing practice of relating to yourself kindly and fairly](https://accelerate.uofuhealth.utah.edu/resilience/how-to-practice-self-compassion-for-resilience-and-well-being)”) is a powerful tool for developing resilience. It involves treating oneself with kindness and understanding, especially during difficult times.

Researchers have identified [three components](https://selfcompassion.web.unc.edu/what-is-self-compassion/the-three-components-of-self-compassion/) of self-compassion: mindfulness, self-kindness, and common humanity.  Moreover, research indicates that [self-compassion](https://www.fasttrackimpact.com/post/self-compassion-and-kindness) is at the root of health resilience. By practicing self-compassion, individuals can reduce stress, improve overall well-being, and develop a more stable self-esteem. This approach helps people bounce back from setbacks more quickly and effectively.

**Finding Meaning in Adversity**

Finding meaning in challenging experiences is a key aspect of developing resilience. When faced with adversity, individuals often search for ways to make sense of their experiences. This process can lead to personal growth and a deeper understanding of oneself and the world. By reframing difficult situations and looking for potential benefits or lessons learned, people can [transform adversity](https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/blog/the-science-of-adversity/201606/5-ways-to-find-meaning-in-life-following-adversity) into opportunities for growth and development.  This ability to find meaning in challenging circumstances contributes significantly to building psychological resilience and adapting to life’s misfortunes.

**Humor**

[Humor](https://positivepsychology.com/humor-psychology/) is associated with greater wellbeing and resilience.  A significant body of research and psychological theory suggests that humor is an important [coping mechanism](https://psychcentral.com/lib/humor-as-weapon-shield-and-psychological-salve), a sophistical psychological [defense](https://psychologyfanatic.com/humor-defense-mechanism/), and an important [character strength](https://ir.lib.uwo.ca/etd/1681/). [Utilizing humor](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29480784/) is a form of [cognitive reappraisal](https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/basics/cognitive-reappraisal); is used by people of all ages (children as well as adults and the elderly); and supports coping strategies to address a range of difficult situations including [daily and chronic stressors](https://www.healthline.com/health/stress/acute-vs-chronic-stress).  Humor seems to work primarily by relieving pent-up pressure, with [laugher](https://www.mayoclinic.org/healthy-lifestyle/stress-management/in-depth/stress-relief/art-20044456) providing an outlet for mental distress.  In many studies, those individuals who were found to use humor more than others generally scored higher on a range of [health status factors](https://www.verywellmind.com/health-benefits-of-humor-and-laughter-5101137) including [immune functioning](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2686627/).

**Part VI. How Psychotherapy Supports Resilience and Coping**

[Psychotherapy has the potential](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC6732704/) to enhance resilience and coping skills, equipping individuals with the capacity to navigate life’s challenges with greater confidence.

Many clients enter treatment with an expressed interest in developing their coping skills but, unfortunately, resilience and coping are often not a direct goal of psychotherapy.  This gap at the outset of treatment is interesting to note given that nearly a century ago, [Anna Freud](https://www.verywellmind.com/anna-freud-biography-1895-1982-2795536), in her pioneering work on developing [ego psychology](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/ego-psychology) and its corresponding model of therapeutic intervention, emphasized the need for therapy to bolster psychological strategies and [defenses mechanisms](https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/basics/defense-mechanisms) to promote [*emotional equilibrium*](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29480784/).  It has been suggested by clinical experts that “[building personal strengths” associated with resilience “should be more integrated into models of psychological treatment](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29480784/).”

Psychotherapy does indeed offer a safe space that allows for the close examination of patterned ways in which clients emotionally react to stress and attempt to respond to it, perhaps not effectively as possible.  Recognizing and altering unhelpful coping strategies mobilizes the treatment process and sets the stage for additional gains the client may be able to make.

To advance the client’s resilience, four interrelated goals in psychotherapy are typically pursued:

* Identifying resilience deficits
* Building psychological flexibility
* Strengthening social connections
* Cultivating mind-body resilience

**Identifying Resilience Deficits**

***Recognizing Maladaptive Coping Strategies.*** Psychotherapy plays a crucial role in identifying resilience deficits by recognizing [maladaptive coping strategies](https://positivepsychology.com/maladaptive-coping/). These unhealthy strategies, such as substance abuse, [self-sabotage](https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/basics/self-sabotage), avoidance, and negative self-talk, may provide some temporary relief but usually lead to more significant negative long-term consequences.  Patients who resort to maladaptive coping often engage in risky behaviors like excessive alcohol use, which can exacerbate physical and mental health problems. In addition, [emotional eating](https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/blog/finding-new-home/201901/why-we-engage-in-emotional-eating), overworking, and attempts to regulate one’s emotions through [primitive psychological defenses](https://www.verywellmind.com/what-are-defense-mechanisms-5213880) such as denial and suppression are common maladaptive strategies that contribute to poor mental health outcomes including anxiety, depression, and chaotic interpersonal relationships.

***Assessing Stress Vulnerability.*** Assessing clients’ *stress vulnerability* profile can have a profound impact on developing their resilience. The [Stress-Vulnerability Model](https://www.verywellmind.com/what-is-the-stress-vulnerability-model-history-elements-6831765) conceptualizes one’s likelihood for developing a psychiatric disorder as being related to the interaction of three factors:

* biological vulnerability (one’s genetic predisposition for psychiatric disorder)
* stress (life stressors such as death of a loved one, loss of a job, etc.)
* protective/resilience factors (factors that reduce the impact of biological vulnerability and stress such as a good social support system)

In this model, prolonged exposure to moderate stressors or to one dramatic stressor may “turn on” or “activate” one’s underlying vulnerability (often referred to as the “[diathesis](https://www.choosingtherapy.com/diathesis-stress-model/)”).  This activation is then regulated by the resilience factors present.  If, for example, the existing resilience factors are overwhelmed by the threshold of activation, emotional, behavioral, and cognitive maladjustment may result.  As you can see, this model clarifies how individuals with higher levels of biological vulnerability and stress may require numerous (and/or more potent) resilience factors to help them overcome adversities.  With this model as a tool, psychotherapy aims to reduce the stress present in the client’s life and to increase the range and intensity of available resilience factors.

***Understanding Personal Trauma History****.*  Clients’ understanding and cognitive/emotional construction or “[narrative](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4217123/)” (meaning-making comprehension) of their trauma history has a significant influence on their resilience. Research supports the notion that one’s [trauma history](https://www.healthline.com/health/mental-health/trauma-related-disorders#types) contributes to the risk of developing PTSD as well as a range of other psychiatric and psychosocial/relationship problems. Individuals who have experienced chronic stress or trauma, particularly during childhood, tend to rely on [cognitive and behavioral avoidance](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0165032723008686) as a coping mechanism. Avoidance, while potentially adaptive in the short term for chronic stress, does not equip individuals to cope effectively with various life circumstances and may place them at risk for mental health problems as well as unstable and unfulfilling relationships. Psychotherapy helps in exploring and processing these past experiences to build more adaptive coping strategies and enhance resilience.

**Building Psychological Flexibility**

***Accepting Difficult Emotions.*** Psychological flexibility (“[the capacity for being in contact with the present and acting on long-term goals rather than short-term urges](https://www.verywellmind.com/psychological-flexibility-7509628)”) plays a crucial role in enhancing resilience and coping skills and is a core concept of [Acceptance and Commitment Therapy](https://www.verywellmind.com/acceptance-commitment-therapy-gad-1393175) (ACT) developed by Steven C. Hayes.  A key aspect of building psychological flexibility involves accepting difficult emotions. This requires individuals to *turn toward* their emotions with acceptance, acknowledging and embracing them rather than pushing them away. By practicing mindfulness, people can calm themselves and soothe their inner turmoil.  Many approaches and techniques aimed at developing mindfulness emphasize the key fact that emotions are [*impermanent*](https://www.spiritualityhealth.com/articles/2019/04/23/the-impermanence-of-emotions), i.e., arising and dissipating, and, therefore, not warranting “attachment” to them.  This premise allows individuals to witness and observe their emotions with kind attention and patience, allowing for emotions to evolve, change, and, in many cases, evaporate.

***Clarifying Personal Values.*** Another vital component of building psychological flexibility is clarifying personal values. This process involves identifying [*core beliefs*](https://psychcentral.com/health/core-beliefs-examples) that guide the client’s decision-making and behavior. By exploring and defining their values, clients can develop a clearer sense of direction, resolve difficult decision-making conundrums, experience greater vitality and motivation, and increase their probability of goal attainment. The recognition of personal values that psychotherapy offers solidifies clients’ deeply personal moral and ethical framework that can then be referred to again and again in times of stress, even without the presence of obvious reinforcers.

***Committing to Meaningful Action.*** The final step in building psychological flexibility involves committing to [meaningful action](https://bayareacbtcenter.com/values-domains-and-values-based-actions-in-act-therapy/). This entails engaging in behaviors that align with one’s values and long-term goals, even in the presence of fear, anxiety, and uncertainty.  Developing a clear sense of one’s own values provides a self-directed purpose and offers a “why” to fuel the “how” and the “what” of goal pursuit. This commitment to value-oriented behaviors can lead to increased resilience and improved quality of life.

**Strengthening Social Connections**

Psychotherapy plays a crucial role in enhancing resilience by strengthening [social connections](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC9560615/). This process involves improving communication skills, developing empathy and trust, and creating and maintaining support systems.

Many clients benefit from exploring these capacities in treatment under the broader umbrella of [emotional intelligence](https://www.helpguide.org/mental-health/wellbeing/emotional-intelligence-eq) (EQ).  EQ emphasizes four skill domains: self-management (regulating emotions and behaviors), self-awareness (recognizing the interaction of one’s thoughts, feelings and behaviors), social awareness (developing empathy and attunement skills and recognizing power/hierarchical group dynamics) and relationship management (forming and maintaining good relationships).

Overall, psychotherapy helps clients understand their own feelings, and the feelings of others, each being crucial for the development of strong, supportive relationships.  Clients also learn how to approach relationships more authentically and with appropriate boundaries, as prior failed romantic relationships and dysfunctional family relationships early in development may have led to [narcissistic](https://thriveworks.com/help-with/narcissism/12-traits-of-a-narcissist) or [co-dependent](https://psychcentral.com/lib/symptoms-signs-of-codependency) tendencies.  Increasing the quality of clients’ social connections contributes to improved mental health, better stress management, and increased emotional regulation, all of which are crucial components of resilience.

**Cultivating Mind-Body Resilience**

***Integrating Relaxation Techniques.*** [Relaxation techniques](https://www.health.harvard.edu/mind-and-mood/six-relaxation-techniques-to-reduce-stress) play a crucial role in cultivating [mind-body resilience](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0167494323000298). These practices help individuals manage stress, enhance emotional regulation, and build inner strength. By incorporating relaxation methods into daily routines, clients can develop effective coping skills to adapt to life’s misfortunes. Techniques such as [progressive muscle relaxation](https://www.webmd.com/sleep-disorders/muscle-relaxation-for-stress-insomnia) and [autogenic relaxation](https://www.verywellmind.com/how-to-practice-autogenic-training-for-relaxation-3024387) focus on reducing muscle tension and promoting a sense of calm. These approaches enable clients to become more awareness of physical sensations, fostering a stronger connection between mind and body.

***Promoting Physical Wellness.*** [Physical wellness](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC9095527/) has a profound impact on psychological resilience. Regular exercise and maintaining overall fitness contribute to stress resilience and improved mental health.  [Studies](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/355143187_The_impact_of_physical_fitness_on_resilience_to_modern_life_stress_and_the_mediating_role_of_general_self-efficacy) have shown that muscular and self-perceived fitness are positively associated with stress resilience. enriched_points.expanded_content: Emotional resilience involves recognizing and managing emotions. It allows individuals to adapt to stress, process challenges, and maintain a balanced perspective during adversity.
Strong social connections provide emotional support and practical assistance. They enhance resilience by fostering a sense of belonging and reducing feelings of isolation during difficult times.
Effective coping mechanisms include problem-solving, emotional regulation, and seeking support. These strategies empower individuals to navigate stressors and bounce back from setbacks.
A positive outlook entails viewing challenges as opportunities for growth. This mindset fosters optimism, enabling individuals to remain motivated and resilient despite difficulties.
Supportive relationships are crucial for resilience. They provide emotional security, foster healthy communication, and help individuals feel understood and valued during life’s challenges.
